Relative     Clauses      Section 21 grammar •  237   relatives: advanced points For more about how-clauses, ►    492.

For details of (he use of whoever, whatever and other words ending in -ever, ►    252.

Look at how he treats me. (= ... the way in which  ...) Whenever you want to come is fine with me. (= Any day that...) (= ... the time when ...) We've bought a cottage in the country for when we retire.

I often think about where I met you. (= ... the place where ...) This is for whoever wants it. (= ... any person that wants it.) when, whenever and how.

Other words that can be used like this are whoever, whichever, where, wherever, Take whatever you want. (= ... anything that you want.) Whatever can be used rather like what, as noun + relative pronoun together.

10 whatever,          whoever,         etc the majority of whom  the youngest of whom   the last of which a number of whom   three of which      half of which superlatives, with first, second, etc, and with last.

This structure is also possible with other expressions of quantity, with She had a teddy bear, both of whose eyes were missing.

The  determiner most often comes before of which/ whom/whose, but can both, several, enough, many, few) can be used with of whom, of which and of In non-identifying clauses, quantifying determiners (e.g. some, any, none, all, 9 some      of   whom,       none      of   which,       etc For more about prepositions at the ends of clauses, ►    209.

... with who he worked) not ... the people with whom he worked, (     ... with that he worked; That is not used after prepositions, and who is unusual.

This is the room (that) I was born in. (informal) - This is the room in which I was born, (formal) He was respected by the people (that) he worked with, (informal) - He was respected by the people with whom he worked, (formal) ends of relative clauses (more informal). Compare: Prepositions can come  either before relative pronouns (more formal) or at the 8  position      of  prepositions For more about place, ►    554. For way, ►    620. For reason, ►    567.

The reason (that) you're so bad-tempered is that you're hungry.

The  same thing happens with why after reason.

Do you know a way (that) you can earn money without working?

I didn't like the way (that) she spoke to me.

After way, in which can be replaced by that or dropped in an informal style.

grammar •  237   relatives: advanced points not ... unless he had neighbours to quarrel with, (     .. .whom to quarrel with.) (sor.. . which to play in;) ... so that the children would have a garden to play in.

preposition without a relative pronoun.

This structure is rather formal, and it is more common to use infinitive + He was miserable unless he had neighbours with whom to quarrel.
